note: Sourced from Clark Gregor's campaign site (clarkgregor.com), City of Plymouth project pages (plymouthmn.gov), Plymouth city council coverage, and local news (Sun Sailor / hometownsource.com, CCX Media, Star Tribune, Roers Companies). Locations are geocoded from the street/intersection named in the source and are approximate for anything short of a full street address. Items marked 'proposed' are not yet built or funded. Where a source documents Gregor's individual vote it is noted; otherwise items reflect actions the council he serves on took during his tenure.

bio.role: Deputy Mayor of Plymouth (2026); At-Large City Council Member (2022–present)
bio.priorService: Planning Commission (2017–present); Charter Commission (2019–2022); Environmental Quality Committee, chair 2020–2021; founded the Plymouth Environmental Academy
bio.background: Marketing strategist at Thrivent; lifelong Plymouth-area resident (Wayzata schools); B.A. Public Communication & Political Science, American University; M.B.A. Communication Management, Fordham University
bio.committees: Environmental Quality Committee liaison; Hello! Plymouth DMO Advisory Board; League of Minnesota Cities Housing Task Force and Improving Service Delivery Policy Committee

items.title: Approved $20M in bonds for 212 affordable apartments
items.location: 5905 Sycamore Lane N — The Boulevard (former Prudential site)
items.summary: Voted with a unanimous council to approve $20 million in housing revenue bonds for the Melrose Apartments — 212 units, 100% affordable at 60% AMI (six at 30% AMI) — at no cost to the city budget. The affordable-housing anchor of the larger Boulevard redevelopment.

items.title: Approved the 75-acre Boulevard redevelopment of the old Prudential site
items.vote: Council approved rezoning, PUD plan & plat
items.location: I-494 & Bass Lake Road (former Prudential campus)
items.summary: Backed the master plan turning the vacant 75-acre Prudential campus into The Boulevard — roughly 925 homes (with affordable units), a new Coborn's grocery, a Summit Orthopedics clinic and surgery center (broke ground October 2024), retail, and a new ~13-acre public park. Melrose is its affordable-housing piece.

items.title: Approved 352 new apartments on Harbor Lane
items.location: Harbor Lane N, north of Highway 55 (approximate)
items.summary: Part of the 4-1 council majority approving Doran Companies' two-building, 352-unit apartment development off Harbor Lane. First phase is due in 2026.

items.title: Approved a 342-home mixed-use project on Highway 55
items.location: North of Highway 55, south of 10th Avenue N (not pinned — location approximate)
items.summary: Supported Dietrich Development's 342-home project — 330 apartments plus 12 townhomes and 5,000 sq ft of retail — along the Highway 55 corridor.

items.title: Advanced the Plymouth Plaza mixed-use redevelopment
items.summary: Backed the redevelopment of the aging Plymouth Plaza retail strip into a 6.5-acre mixed-use project — a 195-unit apartment building (20% affordable at 60% AMI) plus new retail — supported by tax-increment financing.

items.title: Kept the Four Seasons Mall site redevelopment moving
items.location: Former Four Seasons Mall, Lancaster Lane N
items.summary: Voted on the council resolution keeping the long-stalled Four Seasons Mall redevelopment on track — a plan for hundreds of new apartments plus a 255-unit, six-story senior-living building on a site vacant for years.

items.title: Backed the Belterra mixed-income development
items.summary: Supported the Belterra development — 207 units with 20% affordable at 60% AMI, plus retail. Opens late 2026.

items.title: Voted to approve a church campus, with real conditions attached
items.summary: After the council initially rejected the site plan 6-1, Gregor was part of the 4-2 vote to approve Eagle Brook Church's campus with added conditions — a secondary vehicle entrance, a softer building design, and landscaping changes — resolving a threatened lawsuit. He publicly explained his reasoning and committed to Pride events and recognizing welcoming businesses.

items.title: Opened a rebuilt Fire Station 2 in east Plymouth
items.location: Fire Station 2, Old Rockford Road area (approximate)
items.summary: Cut the ribbon on a fully rebuilt Fire Station 2 — a modern facility built to support Plymouth's hybrid full-time / paid-on-call firefighting model in the eastern half of the city.

items.title: Kept the Station 73 transit/safety project on budget
items.summary: Voted with a unanimous council to approve a change order covering unexpected wetland cracking on the ~$23M, DOT-grant-funded Station 73 project — two roundabouts, a pedestrian underpass, and a transit park-and-ride — keeping it within budget using contingency funds.

items.title: Approved the Chankahda Trail reconstruction
items.location: Chankahda Trail, Vicksburg Lane to Northwest Blvd
items.summary: Backed the reconstruction of Chankahda Trail — a full rebuild with new curb and gutter, a roundabout at Teakwood Lane, a trail, and water-main and storm-sewer replacement across a busy northern corridor.

items.title: Added roundabouts to slow traffic on Plymouth Boulevard
items.location: Plymouth Boulevard, Rockford Road to Highway 55
items.summary: Supported the Plymouth Boulevard improvement project — roundabouts to slow vehicle speeds, safer pedestrian crossings, and better trail connections through the civic core near City Hall.

items.title: Rejected an over-budget bid rather than overpay
items.summary: Council rejected the sole construction bid on the Peony Lane and 10th Avenue rehabilitation project after it came in 22% over the engineer's estimate — mill/overlay, ADA ramps, curb and gutter, and railroad bridge work — deferring to the 2027–2028 Capital Improvement Program instead of overpaying.

items.title: Cut the ribbon on the finished Northwest Greenway
items.location: Northwest Greenway, west of Juneau Lane to 57th Ave
items.summary: Marked completion of the sixth and final phase of the 20-year, $9 million Northwest Greenway trail project — two new boardwalks, a rest stop, and a park-and-ride, finishing 7 miles of trail across 315 acres.

items.title: Approved the Lake Camelot Park master plan
items.summary: Approved a multi-phase, $2M+ renovation of Lake Camelot Park — north and south boardwalk replacement, a new restroom building, tree work, and forest health improvements running 2025–2026.

items.title: Approved the South Shore Park renovation
items.summary: Backed a full renovation of South Shore Park — a new playground, a relocated basketball court, trail replacement, a drinking fountain, and bike racks.

items.title: Extended the Plymouth Creek trail and boardwalk
items.location: Plymouth Creek Center to Hilde Performance Center
items.summary: Supported a new trail and boardwalk segment connecting the Plymouth Community Center to the Hilde Performance Center through the Plymouth Creek corridor, with an ADA-compliant realignment near the Ice Center.

items.title: Helped clear three Plymouth lakes off the state impaired-waters list
items.location: Schmidt Lake, Pomerleau Lake & Bass Lake
items.summary: As the council's Environmental Quality liaison, backed restoration work — smart salting, stream restoration, alum treatments, and rain gardens — that removed Schmidt Lake, Pomerleau Lake, and Bass Lake from Minnesota's list of impaired waters.

items.title: Voted for a resolution on federal immigration enforcement
items.summary: Voted with a unanimous council to pass a resolution acknowledging Plymouth residents' fear over federal immigration enforcement ("Operation Metro Surge"), directing funds to community organizations and joining the Cities for Safe and Stable Communities coalition. Gregor: "Safety and dignity are not competing priorities."

items.title: Held Plymouth's tax rate lowest among peer suburbs
items.summary: Voted with a unanimous council to set the 2026 preliminary levy at 7.3% — an estimated ~$182/year on a $500K home — while keeping Plymouth's the lowest tax rate of any comparable Hennepin County suburb (45,000+ residents) since 2017.

items.title: Launched Minnesota's first AI-powered smart tree inventory
items.summary: As the council's liaison to the Environmental Quality Committee, championed a first-in-Minnesota AI inventory of roughly 40,000 city trees to track tree health and guide the urban forest citywide.

items.title: Drove Plymouth's 10-year Climate Action & Resiliency Plan
items.summary: Ran listening sessions and shepherded the city's first Climate Action and Resiliency Plan — a 10-year roadmap for lakes, air, and resilience — through public feedback in 2025.

items.title: Helped 400+ families through home-rehab and passed the 4d incentive
items.summary: Over three years on the city's Housing Task Force, helped connect more than 400 families to the city's home-rehabilitation program and adopted a 4d incentive program in early 2026 to keep rents affordable at properties like Plymouth Colony.

items.title: Proposed Play It Forward Plymouth — a $135M rec investment
items.summary: Presented Play It Forward Plymouth in the 2026 State of the City address — a proposed ice center renovation, new year-round fieldhouse, and regional sports complex, with a local sales-tax referendum floated to fund it. Not yet approved or funded; a stated priority, not a finished project.

officials: Judy Johnson — former Plymouth Mayor & Met Council
organizations: North Central States Regional Council of Carpenters

platform.title: Growing Plymouth thoughtfully
platform.detail: As Plymouth nears build-out, be deliberate about how and where the city grows next.

platform.title: Keeping Plymouth safe
platform.detail: Steadfast support for police, firefighters, and first responders.

platform.title: Housing for every stage of life
platform.detail: Continue expanding housing options across the spectrum, as pursued over the last four years.

platform.title: Government that listens — and shows its work
platform.detail: Fiscal responsibility paired with greater resident involvement in decisions.

platform.title: A strong local economy
platform.detail: Support local businesses and economic opportunity.

platform.title: A Plymouth for everyone
platform.detail: Every resident welcomed, with a voice in the community's future.

platform.title: Protecting lakes, air, and the future
platform.detail: Protect natural resources while preparing the city financially for what's ahead.

platform.title: Parks and recreation for every generation
platform.detail: Maintain and enhance facilities, parks, trails, and programming.
